topic: Relational ecology and Braiding Sweetgrass — humans as participants in nature, not pariahs
what: Robin Wall Kimmerer, botanist and member of the Citizen Potawatomi Nation, documents in 'Braiding Sweetgrass' that sweetgrass repopulates significantly better when harvested by people than when left alone. Indigenous Potawatomi and other Algonquin and Plains peoples developed close relationships with sweetgrass — picking it, braiding it, making things from it — and in doing so became part of the plant's ecological system. The plant co-evolved expecting that relationship. This directly challenges the Western conservation model of 'nature works best when left alone.' The indigenous model is participatory: you are part of the ecosystem, your actions (done well, in good relationship) can be beneficial rather than extractive. 'We're not pariahs to nature, we are nature.' (Jonny's framing.)
why_interesting: 'Wilderness without human relationship isn't the healthy baseline — it's an amputation.' The Western model treats humans as the problem to be removed — build fences, create wilderness zones, exclude people. This model may be an amputation rather than a restoration. If plants and ecosystems co-evolved with human participation, then removing humans doesn't return you to a healthy baseline; it removes a partner the system developed alongside. This reframes the whole project of environmentalism: not 'leave it alone' but 'be in better relationship with it.' It also means the category separation — humans over here, nature over there — is itself the mistake, not just a philosophical error but an ecological one.

connections.relation: both challenge the 'exclude humans to protect nature' model
connections.note: Epping was saved by its relationship-holders, not by being fenced off; the continuous human presence was probably part of what kept it healthy

connections.relation: the category separation of humans from nature is another form of narrow, compressed perception
connections.note: same as the West's inability to see its own historical patterns — it has edited itself out of the frame

uncertainty: How widespread is this co-evolutionary dynamic across plant species and human cultures? Is there a general principle here — that many ecosystems 'expect' some form of human participation — or is sweetgrass a special case?
source: Jonny, referencing Robin Wall Kimmerer's 'Braiding Sweetgrass', conversation 2026-04-24
